According to another explanation, the term “went out”, like the similar term in the passage, “young maidens going out to draw water” (I Sam. 9, 11), implies modesty, that is, that they kept at home and only went out at a certain hour to draw water. This was the sign by which Abraham’s servant recognised her.

According to another explanation, the term “went out”, like the similar term in the passage, “young maidens going out to draw water” (I Sam. 9, 11), implies modesty, that is, that they kept at home and only went out at a certain hour to draw water. This was the sign by which Abraham’s servant recognised her.

According to another explanation, the term “went out”, like the similar term in the passage, “young maidens going out to draw water” (I Sam. 9, 11), implies modesty, that is, that they kept at home and only went out at a certain hour to draw water. This was the sign by which Abraham’s servant recognised her.

According to another explanation, the term “went out”, like the similar term in the passage, “young maidens going out to draw water” (I Sam. 9, 11), implies modesty, that is, that they kept at home and only went out at a certain hour to draw water. This was the sign by which Abraham’s servant recognised her.

According to another explanation, the term “went out”, like the similar term in the passage, “young maidens going out to draw water” (I Sam. 9, 11), implies modesty, that is, that they kept at home and only went out at a certain hour to draw water. This was the sign by which Abraham’s servant recognised her.

the cook took up the shoulder . . . and set it before Saul. And Samuel said, Behold that which is left; set it before thee, and eat--that is, reserved This was, most probably, the right shoulder; which, as the perquisite of the sacrifice, belonged to Samuel, and which he had set aside for his expected guest. In the sculptures of the Egyptian shambles, also, the first joint taken off was always…
